result:
I find myself thinking about the art of noticing.

So much of modern life feels like a scroll, a swipe, a blur of passing content. We are conditioned to absorb, react, and move on. But lately, I’ve been trying to be a curator of the quiet, the tiny, and the apparently insignificant.

For instance, the way morning light hits a glass of water on a wooden table—it throws a trembling, liquid rhombus of gold onto the grain. For that one minute, it is the most important sculpture in the world. The sound of a pen on paper, not the frantic scribble of notes, but the slow, deliberate forming of a single word. The *scratch-whisper* of it. The particular sigh a book makes when you open it for the first time, a release of trapped paper-breath that smells like potential.

I think about how we’ve externalized our attention. Our memories are in clouds, our opinions are pre-packaged, our wonder is often outsourced to influencers in beautiful places. We are taught to document the moment to prove we had it, rather than to sink our teeth into the marrow of the experience itself.

There’s a rebellion in stillness. In deciding that the most radical thing you can do today is to watch a spider engineer its universe between two fence posts, or to follow the journey of a single raindrop down a windowpane. To listen to a friend’s story without mentally formulating your response. To feel the full weight of a feeling—boredom, melancholy, a silly joy—without immediately reaching for a device to blunt its edge.

I’m fascinated by the concept of “deep time,” both geological and personal. The 300-million-year journey of the coal that became the diamond. The 80-year journey of the love letters in my grandmother’s attic, the ink fading but the tenderness still palpable. My own life feels like a single, bright stitch in a vast, dark tapestry. That used to terrify me; now, it’s a strange comfort. My job isn’t to be the whole picture. My job is to be a vivid, honest stitch.

Maybe this is all just a long-winded way of saying I’m learning to fall in love with the world again. Not the loud, shouting world of headlines and algorithms, but the secret one. The one that exists in the pause between two heartbeats, in the geometry of a cracked pavement, in the silent, steadfast companionship of a moon that has witnessed every human sorrow and triumph without ever saying a word.

It’s a practice. Some days I’m blind and frantic. But on the good days, I remember to look. And the world, in its infinite, patient generosity, is always there, waiting to be seen.
